title: A Trans Dad Interrogates the Gendered Experience of Domestic Life
description:
I am a big fan of Krys Malcolm Belc’s writing because he takes binaries like queer:straight male:female and turns them on their heads. In his latest memoir, What I Made for Dinner, he seems to like to live in the gray area, as the title of his essay “I’m a Trans Man, but I’ve Become My […]

The post A Trans Dad Interrogates the Gendered Experience of
hook: A trans dad writes about cooking dinner and discovers it's the most radical act available to him—domestic life as gender trouble.

title: The Healing Power of Watching the Boys of ‘Jackass’ Do Incredibly Stupid Things
description: "Johnny Knoxville and his band of merry pranksters got me through one of the worst weeks of my life."
hook: Jackass wasn't just stupid—it was a lifeline. Why watching people get hurt became the antidote to unbearable weeks.

title: Madonna – Confessions II
description:
Madonna

Confessions II

The restlessly inventive artist for once looks back, but boy has she ever earnt it

For an artist defined by perpetual reinvention, the most striking aspect of Confessions II is Madonna’s resolve to look back at a career that reshaped pop music. Steeped in nostalgic, self-referential anecdotes, the album carries
hook: Madonna's new album finds her looking backward for once, and she's earned every second of that reflection.

title: The Kiss by Katie Barclay review – a history of passion, power and puckering up
description:
From heads of state to foot-kissing knights, Hollywood romcoms to sex, a fascinating story of the gesture that for centuries has brought people together

If, on a European holiday, you get flustered greeting people – should you kiss? how many times? – spare a thought for Dutch theologian Desiderius Erasmus. Visiting England in 1499, he found a nation of enthusiastic kissers. “Wherever you go, you are received on all hands with kisses; when you take leave you are dismissed with kisses,” h
hook: The kiss shaped empires and scandals. A historian traces how one gesture became the story of power, desire, and who gets to decide intimacy.

title: World Cupcophony
description: On the field, every World Cup is a contest of idioms: between the Spanish-influenced juego de posición and post-German Gegenpressing, between philosophies that rely on holding the ball and those that assert themselves by working without it, between the set piece-heavy structuralism now fashionable in the English Premier League and the crashing unpredictabil
hook: World Cup soccer is a battle between competing philosophies—Spanish geometry versus German chaos. Every tournament asks: which vision wins?
